--- title: "Model 1 ‚Äî XGBoost" subtitle: "Gradient-boosted trees, the Kaggle workhorse" --- > **Where we are:** First of three model documents in the > [Titanic ‚Äî Tidymodels Tour](../index.qmd). All three follow the *same* > structure ‚Äî recipe ‚Üí spec ‚Üí workflow ‚Üí tune ‚Üí finalize ‚Üí evaluate ‚Üí save ‚Äî so > once you've read this one, [Random Forest](03-random-forest.qmd) and > [Neural Net](04-neural-net.qmd) are quick to skim for the differences. The > features come from the [EDA](01-eda.qmd) and the shared `make_recipe()`. ## Why XGBoost first? Gradient-boosted decision trees are the default first reach on tabular Kaggle problems. They handle mixed numeric/categorical features, capture interactions (like sex √ó class) automatically, are robust to monotone transforms, and tune to strong accuracy with modest effort. We tune four of the most impactful knobs. ## Setup ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Project setup: tidyverse + tidymodels, seed = 42, paths, parallel backend. source(here::here("R", "00_setup.R")) # Shared feature engineering: defines make_recipe(data). source(here("R", "02_features.R")) # Attach the libraries used throughout this document. library(tidyverse) # dplyr, ggplot2, readr, stringr, tibble, tidyr, purrr library(tidymodels) # recipes, parsnip, workflows, tune, yardstick, rsample, dials, broom library(here) # path construction library(vip) # variable importance plots library(xgboost) # the engine ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} train <- read_csv(here("data", "raw", "train.csv")) |> # Project-wide convention: Survived is a factor, levels c("0","1"), # positive class = "1" (so metrics use event_level = "second"). mutate(Survived = factor(Survived, levels = c("0", "1"))) ``` ::: ## The recipe The shared recipe encodes everything we justified in the [EDA](01-eda.qmd): impute `Age`/`Embarked`, derive `FamilySize`, extract `Title`, log-transform `Fare`, dummy-encode factors, drop identifiers. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} xgb_recipe <- make_recipe(train) xgb_recipe ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` ‚îÄ‚îÄ Recipe ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` ‚îÄ‚îÄ Inputs ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Number of variables by role ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` outcome: 1 predictor: 10 id: 1 ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` ‚îÄ‚îÄ Operations ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` ‚Ä¢ Variable mutation for: str_extract(Name, "(?<=, )[A-Za-z]+(?=\\.)"), ... ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` ‚Ä¢ Collapsing factor levels for: Title ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` ‚Ä¢ Variable mutation for: SibSp + Parch + 1L, ... ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` ‚Ä¢ Variable mutation for: str_sub(Cabin, 1, 1), ... ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` ‚Ä¢ Variables removed: Name, Ticket, Cabin ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` ‚Ä¢ K-nearest neighbor imputation for: Age ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` ‚Ä¢ Mode imputation for: Embarked ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` ‚Ä¢ Median imputation for: Fare ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` ‚Ä¢ Variable mutation for: as.character(Pclass) ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` ‚Ä¢ Novel factor level assignment for: all_nominal_predictors() ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` ‚Ä¢ Dummy variables from: all_nominal_predictors() ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` ‚Ä¢ Zero variance filter on: all_predictors() ``` ::: ::: ## The model specification We mark the four tuning parameters with `tune()`. tidymodels will fill them in during the grid search. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} xgb_spec <- boost_tree( trees = tune(), tree_depth = tune(), learn_rate = tune(), min_n = tune() ) |> set_engine("xgboost") |> set_mode("classification") xgb_spec ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Boosted Tree Model Specification (classification) Main Arguments: trees = tune() min_n = tune() tree_depth = tune() learn_rate = tune() Computational engine: xgboost ``` ::: ::: ## The workflow A `workflow` bundles the recipe and the model so resampling applies the *exact* same preprocessing to every fold ‚Äî no leakage. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} xgb_wf <- workflow() |> add_recipe(xgb_recipe) |> add_model(xgb_spec) ``` ::: ## Tuning with 5-fold cross-validation ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Stratify on the outcome so each fold preserves the ~38% survival rate. set.seed(42) folds <- vfold_cv(train, v = 5, strata = Survived) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # A space-filling Latin-hypercube grid over the four parameters. dials supplies # sensible ranges; we finalize() the ones that depend on the data (none here, # but it's good habit). 20 candidates keeps the demo fast. set.seed(42) xgb_grid <- grid_latin_hypercube( trees(range = c(200L, 1500L)), tree_depth(range = c(2L, 10L)), learn_rate(range = c(-3, -0.5)), # log10 scale ‚Üí 0.001 to ~0.32 min_n(range = c(2L, 40L)), size = 20 ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Warning: `grid_latin_hypercube()` was deprecated in dials 1.3.0. ‚Ñπ Please use `grid_space_filling()` instead. ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Score every candidate on both metrics. event_level = "second" because the # positive class ("survived" = "1") is the SECOND factor level. xgb_metrics <- metric_set(roc_auc, accuracy) set.seed(42) xgb_tuned <- tune_grid( xgb_wf, resamples = folds, grid = xgb_grid, metrics = xgb_metrics, control = control_grid(save_pred = TRUE, event_level = "second") ) ``` ::: ### Tuning results ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} autoplot(xgb_tuned, metric = "roc_auc") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![ROC-AUC across the tuning grid for each hyperparameter (autoplot).](02-xgboost_files/figure-html/tune-plot-1.png){width=864} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # The top candidates by ROC-AUC. show_best(xgb_tuned, metric = "roc_auc", n = 5) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
````` ::: ::: ## Finalize and fit on the full training set ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Pick the best ROC-AUC candidate and lock those hyperparameters in. best_xgb <- select_best(xgb_tuned, metric = "roc_auc") best_xgb ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
````` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} final_xgb_wf <- finalize_workflow(xgb_wf, best_xgb) # Fit the finalized workflow on ALL the training data ‚Äî this is the model we # will ship to Kaggle. set.seed(42) xgb_fit <- fit(final_xgb_wf, data = train) ``` ::: ## Cross-validated performance The fairest estimate of out-of-sample skill is the CV score of the chosen configuration ‚Äî not the fit on the full training data (which overfits by construction). ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Collect the CV metrics for the winning hyperparameters. collect_metrics(xgb_tuned) |> semi_join(best_xgb, by = c("trees", "tree_depth", "learn_rate", "min_n")) |> select(.metric, mean, std_err, n) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
````` ::: ::: ::: {.callout-tip} Copy the `accuracy` and `roc_auc` `mean` values into the results table on the [master document](../index.qmd). ::: ### Confusion matrix Built from the held-out CV predictions for the best configuration ‚Äî so it reflects generalization, not memorization. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} xgb_cv_preds <- collect_predictions(xgb_tuned, parameters = best_xgb) xgb_cv_preds |> conf_mat(truth = Survived, estimate = .pred_class) |> autoplot(type = "heatmap") + labs(title = "XGBoost ‚Äî CV confusion matrix") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Confusion matrix from out-of-fold CV predictions.](02-xgboost_files/figure-html/confusion-1.png){width=480} ::: ::: ### Variable importance ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} xgb_fit |> extract_fit_parsnip() |> vip(num_features = 15, geom = "col", aesthetics = list(fill = "steelblue")) + labs(title = "XGBoost variable importance") + theme_minimal() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Top features by XGBoost gain. Title, Sex, Fare and class dominate.](02-xgboost_files/figure-html/vip-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: The engineered `Title`, plus `Sex`, `Fare`, and class, sit at the top ‚Äî exactly the signal the [EDA](01-eda.qmd) surfaced. Feature engineering earns its keep. ## Save the fitted workflow ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Persist the full fitted workflow (recipe + model) so the submission document # can load it without refitting. Project convention: models/_fit.rds. saveRDS(xgb_fit, file = here("models", "xgboost_fit.rds")) ``` ::: ## Submission Generate this model's leaderboard CSV with the **parameterized** [submission document](submission.qmd): ```bash quarto render docs/submission.qmd -P model:xgboost ``` That loads `models/xgboost_fit.rds`, predicts on `data/raw/test.csv`, writes `models/submission_xgboost.csv` (columns `PassengerId`, `Survived` as 0/1 integers), and prints the exact `kaggle competitions submit` command. See the [Kaggle setup guide](setup-kaggle.md) for authentication. ## Next steps - Compare against the **[Random Forest](03-random-forest.qmd)** ‚Äî a robust, almost-no-tuning baseline. - Then the **[Neural Net](04-neural-net.qmd)**, which needs normalized inputs. - Build the CSV with the **[Submission doc](submission.qmd)**. - Record this model's CV scores in the **[master results table](../index.qmd)**.
